name: Peruvian Lily
description: Peruvian Lily (Alstroemeria spp.), also called Lily of the Incas, is a popular cut flower that is mildly toxic to cats. Unlike true lilies (Lilium), Alstroemeria does not cause acute kidney failure. It contains irritant compounds in the sap that cause gastrointestinal upset when ingested and contact dermatitis when the sap touches skin.
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: unidentified irritant compounds in the sap; not related to the nephrotoxic compounds in true Lilium species
Mechanism: GI irritant; sap contact causes dermatitis through direct tissue irritation
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling; skin or eye contact with sap may cause redness and irritation
No kidney failure risk — a critical difference from Easter and Tiger lilies
Symptoms are mild and self-limiting; contact your vet if ingestion was significant or symptoms persist

symptoms.name: Stomach upset (vomiting and/or diarrhea)
symptoms.notes: The stomach upset caused by Peruvian lilies is considered minor and self-limiting13. Because Peruvian lilies are not "true" lilies, they do not cause acute kidney failure, and actual poisoning is rare despite the plant's frequent appearance in floral bouquets1. 2. Minor Oral Irritation

symptoms.name: Minor signs of oral toxicity or irritation
symptoms.onset: Immediate to shortly after chewing the plant.
symptoms.notes: This minor irritation is caused by the oxalate crystals present in the plant, which act as a physical irritant when the cat chews on the plant material
